channel.title: Radio National Breakfast - Breakfast Wrap
channel.description: Filtered feed containing only Breakfast Wrap episodes from Radio National Breakfast
channel.itunes:author: ABC Radio National

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Why big tobacco was given a secret Senate hearing
item.description:
The federal government and the Greens are demanding answers after a Liberal-led Senate inquiry allowed tobacco giant Philip Morris to deliver evidence behind closed doors yesterday.

Liberal Senator Leah Blyth chairs the committee looking into illegal tobacco in Australia and granted the closed hearing - to the dismay of several health organisations already giving evidence.

We hear from one of the organisation's who gave its evidence publicly. 

Then, what is going on in the Strait of Hormuz? A former US Naval commander shares their insights. 

And we ask the government about gambling reform. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: How Australia conquered curling
item.description:
Is this Australia's Cool Runnings moment?

Australia's mixed team curlers Tahli Gill and Dean Hewitt have claimed world championship gold in Switzerland.

We hear from Dean fresh off the win. 

Then, the Japanese Prime Minister is in town for annual talks, so why is this a particularly important trip from the new leader?

And we'll return to Alice Springs days after the devastation of the death of a five year-old girl. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Grief and anger in Alice Springs
item.description:
As Alice Springs grieves the death of a five-year-old girl missing since the weekend, violence erupts outside the hospital where the accused killer is being held.

We hear from community organisations and police working in town as emotions boil over, and the alleged murderer is moved to Darwin for safety reasons. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Betting on a war
item.description:
What do you know about Polymarket?

We look at the platform letting people put a bet on basically anything - from sport to war. And how its being used to predict attacks in the Middle East. 

Then, ahead of next month's federal budget we hear from Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson. 

And the former Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert discusses the ongoing strikes in Lebanon.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The King's speech
item.description:
Overnight, King Charles followed in his mother's foot steps - telling the joint session of Congress that the bond between the US and UK is irreplaceable and unbreakable.

We'll unpack the historic address and look at how the royal visit is going. 

Then, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has unveiled the government's long-awaited proposal to make tech giants pay for Australian journalism. 

And with the Foreign Minister in China, calls are growing for renewed attention on the ethnic minority Uyghur population.

 Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: 30 years since Port Arthur
item.description:
The Port Arthur massacre is remembered not just for the horror of that day, but for the political and societal response that followed, changing Australia forever.

Hear our interview with then Prime Minister John Howard, who played a major role in reforming the country's gun laws in the wake of the attack. 

We'll also cross to the US to hear from a former FBI agent following the White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting, and find out about a tech titan's court battle for the ages. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Inside the White House Correspondents' Dinner
item.description:
Lax security and entry tickets left in bathrooms. We hear what it was like inside the White House Correspondents' Dinner as a suspected gunman tries to cause chaos at one of the biggest events in Washington. 

Then, how will the attack impact the upcoming royal visit to DC? 

We'll also hear about the widely condemned boos at ANZAC Day ceremonies around the country, from someone who was targeted. 

And what is the impact of the government's major NDIS reform on those who use the services?

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Is the government's gas tax dead?
item.description:
The prime minister is poised to kill off a move to increase taxes on gas giants - echoing the industry's argument that exporters are already paying tens of billions of dollars in taxes each year.

You'll hear from a range of key voices in the gas tax debate.

The United Nations is confident it can get fertiliser shipments through the Strait of Hormuz within seven days, and save millions of people from food insecurity. We speak to the UN boss in charge of the mission.

Back home, fallout from the NDIS reforms continue, as the autism community fears many will be left without support when the federal government cuts 160,000 people from the scheme.

And the new director of Adelaide Writer's Week joins the show.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: What do NDIS families think of these major reforms?
item.description:
The federal government has announced sweeping changes to the National Disability Insurance Scheme, in a bid to bring spending back down to sustainable levels, and clamp down on fraud.

On the program we bring you the reactions of participants and their families, as well as advocates and providers.

You'll also hear from one of the original architects of the NDIS, and Health Minister Mark Butler.

Meanwhile in the Strait of Hormuz, Iran has attacked three vessels, seizing two, as tensions rise in the vital waterway.

It comes as US President Donald Trump claims a ceasefire with Iran has been extended - but without a new date.

We'll unpack the state of the Strait.

 Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Major reforms ahead for NDIS
item.description:
Big changes are coming to the National Disability Insurance Scheme today - with Health Minister Mark Butler expected to announce measures to curb the growing cost of the scheme later today.

We dive into this with one of the original architects of the scheme, and Greens NDIS Minister, Jordon Steele-John.

In the Middle East, US President Donald Trump has announced a ceasefire between the US and Iran, hours before it was set to expire - you'll get the latest on a rapidly evolving situation.

The E-Safety Commissioner will also weigh in on her decision to put online gaming giants on notice, over fears that extremists and sexual predators are using these platforms to radicalise young people.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: British PM apologises over Mandelson scandal
item.description:
US President Donald Trump has offered mixed messages overnight about the path ahead for the U.S. war against Iran, declaring that he is in no rush to end the conflict as the second round of peace talks with Iran hangs in the balance.

Iran is currently refusing to send a delegation to the talks in Pakistan - after the U.S. Navy attacked and seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship near the Strait of Hormuz. 

Meanwhile the UK and France have been leading talks on how to best protect commercial shipping routes, once the Strait of Hormuz is secured.

France's Trade Minister joins the program.

Then, you'll learn more about the state of the Strait from an expert who's spent 30 years as an officer in the UK Royal Navy.

Back home, the global oil crisis is impacting Australia's ability to build the one million homes needed under the national housing accord. Housing Minister Clare O'Neill fronts up.

Meanwhile the government is considering a windfall tax on profits made by gas exports.

It comes as new research finds Japan has earned $8 billion a year on average on imports of Australian gas over the past five years.

And in the UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer has been grilled in parliament over the appointment of the former British ambassador to the United States, Lord Peter Mandelson.

Lord Mandelson was sacked in September, just nine months into the job, when new details emerged about his friendship with the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Sir Starmer has been facing more questions about what he knew and when, after it was revealed Lord Mandelson failed the security vetting process for the ambassadorship. Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: The State of the Strait
item.description:
US President Donald Trump says the U.S. military has taken control of an Iranian-flagged cargo ship by blowing a hole in its engine room. 

It comes after Iran reversed its decision to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran has fired upon multiple vessels at the weekend, with no casualties reported. 

We dive into the state of the Strait with a former merchant mariner.

Then, we look at what this means for global oil markets, and how the diminishing supply of jet fuel could impact Australian airfares.

Then, there's lots to unpack with our federal politicians around the economic fallout of the Iran war. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Trump 'not happy' with Australia - again
item.description:
A 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon has now begun - signalling a pause in Israel's conflict with Iran-backed Hezbollah that has raged in parallel to the war with Iran.

Also, President Donald Trump has lashed out at Australia - again - for not helping re-open the Strait of Hormuz.

Both Defence Minister Richard Marles and his shadow counterpart James Paterson join the program.

It comes as the federal government announces a $53 billion spending boost in defence - but is this enough of an investment? We ask a defence expert.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Refinery fire disrupts Australia's petrol supply
item.description:
A fire has broken out at Viva Energy's Geelong Oil Refinery -  one of Australia's two remaining oil refineries.

Firefighters spent the night battling the fire, and questions are now being asked around the impact on domestic fuel supply.

This comes as senior federal government ministers travel to Asian countries, as they seek assurances on fuel reserves.

Trade Minister Don Farrell joins the program before he heads to Singapore.

Then, key Pakistani mediators are in the Iranian capital trying to revive peace talks between the U-S and Iran.

US and Iran blockades in the Strait of Hormuz remain, but President Donald Trump remains optimistic a deal can be reached.

And the Coalition says it has a plan to restore "integrity and confidence" to Australia's migration system - but experts say there are significant flaws in their policy.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: IMF warns of global recession
item.description:
The International Monetary Fund has warned of significant pressure on the global economy and inflation if the war between the US and Iran is prolonged.

Its worst-case scenario would see a global recession... inflation as high as six per cent... and elevated fuel prices all the way through 2027.

We'll speak to Treasurer Jim Chalamers.

Then, Israel and Lebanon have held their first direct talks in over three decades, with U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio calling it a “historic opportunity”.

But he also made clear no breakthrough agreement was expected, as the conflict -- between the Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah and Israel -- continues.

Back in Australia, the Opposition Leader Angus Taylor has outlined the first part of the Coalition's immigration policy, aiming to reduce migration, increase screening of visa applicants and kick migrants out of the country who do not abide by Australian values.

Finally, we look back at three years of civil war in Sudan, which has seen more than 14-million people displaced.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: President Trump vs Pope Leo
item.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump says the American military has begun a blockade of Iranian ports as part of his effort to force Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

Speaking outside the Oval Office overnight, Mr Trump suggested the U.S. is still willing to engage with Iran, after 21-hour long peace talks failed at the weekend. 

Also, an extraordinary feud is growing between US President Donald Trump and the Pope.

The global leader of the Catholic church has said he plans to continue speaking out against war, despite scathing comments from the US president.

While in Australia, energy prices are on the rise due to the war in the Middle East, but the cost of electricity hasn't seen a similar jump, we'll unpack why that is.

There's also a lot of movement in the defence industry.

You'll hear about the billions of dollars set to be invested in drones, and major changes in the top military.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Trump's US blockade on the Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
Agenda-setting news coverage, breaking news and the most significant stories impacting the lives of all Australians.

First it was Iran imposing a de facto blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, strangling a fifth of the world's oil trade.

Now, US President Donald Trump has vowed to blockade the strait after peace talks with Iran failed to secure an agreement

Iran has demanded the right to collect tolls from vessels travelling through the strait, but Mr Trump says no one who pays them will “have safe passage on the high seas.”

We unpack what this means for the shipping industry, and the legality of a US blockade in the Strait.

Then, the federal government is launching a campaign encouraging Australians to use less fuel where possible. Environment Minister Murray Watt joins the program.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: What is the future of the Iran ceasefire?
item.description:
The Israeli Prime Minister has ordered talks with the Lebanese government, so what does this mean for the ceasefire in the Middle East?

We'll take you on the ground to Beirut and you'll hear from a former senior figure in the US State Department.

The Acting Prime Minister Richard Marles will also weigh in on the future of the Strait of Hormuz, and our Friday political panel will wrap the week.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Lebanon under Israeli bombardment despite Iran ceasefire
item.description:
We hear from the former Lebanese Health Minister, a surgeon today treating an influx of casualties in a Beirut hospital after Israel's deadly strikes. They come despite Israel and the US agreeing to a ceasefire with Iran. 

We unpack why the US and Israel say Lebanon was excluded from the ceasefire with Iran, and what the Australian government's response is - hear our interview with Foreign Minister Penny Wong. 

We also dissect what is happening with the Strait of Hormuz, as there's confusion over whether Iran has reopened it.

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Iran ceasefire and Trump's violent rhetoric
item.description:
The US President agrees to a two week ceasefire if Iran reopens the Strait of Hormuz, only hours after he violently threatens annihilation.

We unpack the gravity of his comments and get immediate analysis on the prospect of this conflict screeching to a halt, right as it was set to escalate. 

Then, the Nationals are set to plant an ideological flag in the ground over the economy. The new leader Matt Canavan fronts up. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Humans go further into space than ever before
item.description:
The Artemis II crew goes further into space than any humans have gone before.

We'll bring you everything you need to know as the capsule goes dark, for a planned blackout on the far side of the moon.

Then, to fuel news predating the current global fuel crisis,  could the life-threatening shortage in Cuba be about to ease?

We'll also look at the latest comments on Iran from US President Donald Trump and his repeated criticism of Australia. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Has the government's gambling response gone far enough?
item.description:
Long-awaited government action on gambling reform will include a partial ban on gambling advertising on television and radio and a total ban in sports venues.

The government says gambling ads will also be banned on social media and streaming platforms unless users are logged in, over 18 and have the option to opt out.

So how much further do some politicians want the government to go?

We'll then hear about the US President's latest threats over Iran, and about a long-awaited threat he has finally followed through on, that will impact the Australian pharmaceutical sector. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Your questions about the war in Iran
item.description:
For weeks we've been asking you to send in your questions about the war in Iran, the geopolitics, and also the economic fallout, particularly around the global fuel crisis and Australia's response. 

So on today's Breakfast Wrap we share the answers to your questions. 25 minutes of experts we've spoken to on Radio National Breakfast since the war began. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Artemis II launches for the Moon
item.description:
It is a historic day for space flight with the Artemis II mission launching this morning from Cape Canaveral in Florida. 

We hear from a former NASA astronaut as the world watches our first return to the Moon in more than 50 years. While this mission won't see astronauts land on the lunar surface, they will voyage around it before returning to Earth in ten days. 

Then, we look at news back home, the PM's address to the nation and developments out of the Middle East. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.

item.title: Breakfast Wrap: Could the Strait of Hormuz stay shut post US-exit?
item.description:
There is growing speculation that the US could exit the war with Iran, with the Strait of Hormuz still remaining effectively closed. 

We'll unpack the comments fueling the speculation, and hear how Europe and how both sides of Australian politics are responding.

Then, we'll turn to domestic affairs - and hear about the latest debates in parliament and tension within the Liberal party. 

Recap the morning's news, politics and global affairs with the Breakfast Wrap.
